content.text: Working memory is the structured, task-relevant state an agent maintains during a session — distinct from the raw conversation history. Where conversation history preserves every exchange verbatim, working memory extracts and organises the facts that matter: what the current task is, what has been decided, what is still open, what subtasks remain. It is the agent's "whiteboard": compact, queryable, and updatable in place.

attrs.alt: Raw conversation history vs working memory: structured object with project name, tech stack, tasks, decisions
attrs.title: Working memory vs raw conversation history

content.text: Working memory solves a practical problem: as a session grows, the relevant facts are scattered across dozens of turns. Injecting the entire history into every prompt is expensive and forces the model to re-parse prose to find current state. Injecting a compact working memory object instead is cheaper, faster, and less error-prone.
Define working memory as a Python dataclass or Pydantic model. Typed fields make the structure explicit and enable validation when the model updates it.
Updating Working Memory via a Tool

tool. This lets it update specific fields as it learns new information, rather than relying on implicit extraction from prose. The tool call is also logged, providing an audit trail of how the agent's understanding evolved.
Injecting Working Memory into the System Prompt

Pass the working memory state as part of the system prompt on every call. Because it is compact, this adds only a few hundred tokens per turn — a fraction of what the full conversation history would cost.
When to Use Working Memory vs. Conversation History
Need to update a single fact
Serialize working memory to JSON between sessions so tasks resume with full context even after a process restart.
Instead of relying on the model to call

voluntarily, you can run a lightweight extraction pass after every assistant turn. Use a cheap model (Haiku) to parse the latest exchange and update the memory object with any new facts, decisions, or task completions it detects. This is useful when the main model is focused on solving the problem and might forget to update memory.

import anthropic, json, re

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def extract_memory_updates(
    mem: WorkingMemory,
    last_user_msg: str,
    last_assistant_msg: str,
) -> WorkingMemory:
    """Run a cheap extraction pass to update working memory from the latest exchange."""
    current_state = mem.to_prompt_string()
    prompt = f"""Current working memory:
{current_state}

Latest exchange:
USER: {last_user_msg[:500]}
ASSISTANT: {last_assistant_msg[:500]}

Extract any NEW information from the exchange that should update working memory.
Respond with a JSON object containing only the fields to change:
{{
  "set_fact": {{}},        // new key-value facts
  "set_decision": {{}},   // new decisions made
  "add_pending": "",      // new task added (one per call; omit if none)
  "complete_task": "",    // task completed (omit if none)
  "add_question": "",     // new open question (omit if none)
  "current_step": ""      // updated current step (omit if unchanged)
}}
Return {{}} if nothing new was learned."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",  # cheap extraction model
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text.strip()
    m = re.search(r"\{.*\}", raw, re.DOTALL)
    if not m:
        return mem

    try:
        updates = json.loads(m.group())
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        return mem

    return apply_memory_update(mem, updates)


# Usage in the chat loop (passive extraction — no update_memory tool needed):
def passive_memory_chat(
    mem: WorkingMemory,
    history: list[dict],
    user_message: str,
) -> tuple[str, WorkingMemory, list[dict]]:
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_message})
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=build_system_prompt(mem),
        messages=history,
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    # Extract memory updates without requiring the model to call a tool
    mem = extract_memory_updates(mem, user_message, reply)
    return reply, mem, history

Complex tasks decompose into subtasks, each with their own local state. Use a stack of working memory objects: push a child context when entering a subtask, pop it when done, and merge relevant results back into the parent. This mirrors how a human engineer switches between "what am I building overall" and "what am I debugging right now".

from copy import deepcopy

class MemoryStack:
    """A stack of WorkingMemory objects for nested task execution."""

    def __init__(self, root: WorkingMemory):
        self._stack: list[WorkingMemory] = [root]

    @property
    def current(self) -> WorkingMemory:
        return self._stack[-1]

    def push_subtask(self, goal: str) -> WorkingMemory:
        """Create a child context for a subtask, inheriting facts from parent."""
        child = WorkingMemory(
            goal=goal,
            current_step="starting",
            facts=deepcopy(self.current.facts),       # inherit facts
            decisions=deepcopy(self.current.decisions), # inherit decisions
        )
        self._stack.append(child)
        return child

    def pop_subtask(self, result_fact: str | None = None) -> WorkingMemory:
        """Complete a subtask and return to the parent context.

        Optionally merge a result fact back into the parent.
        """
        child = self._stack.pop()
        parent = self._stack[-1]

        if result_fact:
            parent.facts[f"subtask_{child.goal[:20]}"] = result_fact

        # Merge errors from child into parent
        parent.errors_encountered.extend(child.errors_encountered)

        return parent


# Example: main task with two subtasks
root_mem = WorkingMemory(goal="Set up a production deployment")
stack = MemoryStack(root_mem)

# Subtask 1
stack.push_subtask("Configure database connection")
# ... run subtask steps ...
stack.pop_subtask(result_fact="db_url=postgres://prod-host:5432/app")

# Subtask 2
stack.push_subtask("Build Docker image")
# ... run subtask steps ...
stack.pop_subtask(result_fact="image=myapp:v1.2.3")

# Back to parent context with results merged
final_mem = stack.current
print(final_mem.facts)  # {'subtask_Configure database ': '...', 'subtask_Build Docker image': '...'}

import pytest
from copy import deepcopy

def test_apply_memory_update_set_fact():
    mem = WorkingMemory(goal="build app")
    updated = apply_memory_update(mem, {"set_fact": {"db_host": "localhost"}})
    assert updated.facts["db_host"] == "localhost"
    assert mem.facts == {}  # original unchanged (deepcopy)

def test_apply_memory_update_complete_task():
    mem = WorkingMemory(pending=["write tests", "deploy"])
    updated = apply_memory_update(mem, {"complete_task": "write tests"})
    assert "write tests" not in updated.pending
    assert "write tests" in updated.completed

def test_apply_memory_update_complete_nonexistent_task():
    mem = WorkingMemory(pending=["deploy"])
    updated = apply_memory_update(mem, {"complete_task": "nonexistent"})
    assert "nonexistent" in updated.completed  # still records it
    assert updated.pending == ["deploy"]        # pending unchanged

def test_to_prompt_string_omits_empty_fields():
    mem = WorkingMemory(goal="write a script", current_step="drafting")
    s = mem.to_prompt_string()
    assert "Goal: write a script" in s
    assert "Known facts" not in s   # empty dict should not appear
    assert "Pending tasks" not in s

def test_memory_stack_push_pop():
    root = WorkingMemory(goal="deploy app", facts={"env": "production"})
    stack = MemoryStack(root)
    child = stack.push_subtask("build image")
    assert child.facts["env"] == "production"  # inherited
    assert child.goal == "build image"
    parent = stack.pop_subtask(result_fact="image_tag=v1.0")
    assert "image_tag" in str(parent.facts)
Working memory is structured, task-relevant state maintained separately from the raw conversation history. Define it as a typed dataclass, give the model an

tool to modify it explicitly, and inject the compact serialised state into the system prompt on every turn. This pattern is most valuable for long-running tasks (50+ turns), multi-step workflows that must track progress, and multi-agent systems where a shared state object is more useful than sharing a full conversation history.
